## [00:00 - 03:06]

Hi, this is Don Crowther. Let's talk about your webinar funnels sales page. So your webinar funnel sales page has to actually work harder than a normal sales page, especially for those who don't know you very well yet. They haven't been through a lot of other kinds of sales materials, prelaunch content and things like that to pre support predispose them to sell. And so this page has to work pretty hard. And this makes that sales page that you send them to very important. So I want to make a distinction here between the two kinds of sales pages that we're going to use during the webinar funnel. So there is the webinar abbreviated sales page. And then there is the webinar funnel sales page. So let's talk about the abbreviated the webinar abbreviated sales page. This is the page that you send people to during your webinar. It's short and sweet. It's devoted towards closing the deal. This is the page that they've just sat through an hour or more of time of you talking about their problems solving making a transformation in their life. So getting all the good feelings that come from that. And then you just spent some time talking about the product. So they've seen pictures of the product, they've heard descriptions of the product. Basically, all the selling messages already been delivered. And so on the abbreviated sales page, all you have to do is just give them that last final bit of information that enables them to be able to make the decision to buy. You don't have to have a lot of sales kind of content on that page. You just did that in a webinar. Now the webinar funnel sales page is the page you send people to who haven't purchased from your previous promotions, your webinar and your replay. And so these are people that many times have not even seen those. And so they've not yet been given the information that they need to have in order to be able to make a decision whether to buy or not. And so this page is longer. It's more detailed. And it's devoted towards making the sale as opposed to the abbreviated was closing the deal. Here you got to make the sale and close the deal. So it's got to work a lot harder. Now there are two options on this page. The first one is to use a sales video only. The second one is to use a complete sales page, ideally including a sales video. So a sales video option is basically a video giving your complete sales message.

## [03:07 - 06:07]

And as you do that, you can't assume that they've actually seen anything about your product prior to this point. Because you're sending this, you're exposing this to people who have not registered for your webinar, have registered webinar and didn't attend it, who left before they got the sales message. There's a lot of people who are seeing this who just have not seen anything about your product. So you have to be fairly long and fairly complete in this sales video, because you have to do all the work to sell them. And you'll want to have a buy button that appears when the price is revealed. Now I will tell you that the sales video is actually not my favorite for this particular sales page. And the reason is that a large percentage of the audience you're going to show this sales page to has already indicated that they're not willing to spend a lot of time watching video. The 70% of them who won't sign up for a webinar, the probably 80% of them who won't watch the replay. So 50% of your more of the people who are going to see this have already said, hey, I don't watch long videos. And so if the only thing you give them to sell them the product is another long video, they've already clearly told you, hey, I don't watch long videos. That's the reason why I actually like the complete sales page option, where in addition to a sales video, though the sales video is optional here, you don't have to have it, but it does add. I also have significant amounts of printed sales material printed on the web page that takes them through. So for those who are not willing to be sold to via video, I'm going to give them a print version of it so that they can get the information the information they need to buy. Now if they do watch the video and want to just click the button after that and buy, I'm more than happy to allow them to do it. But I'm also building a page that's specifically designed to help those who aren't willing to expose themselves to a long video to buy. Now that sales video, it could potentially be a shorter version than you would use on the sales video only page, because it doesn't have to work quite as hard because you got the rest of the text based sales letter below that video. Now there will be on a good complete sales page with the sales video on it. There will be some duplication between the sales video and the text and you don't worry about that. Because most people who come to that page at this page in the webinar funnel are probably not going to

## [06:07 - 09:13]

watch that video all the way through. So I've got no problem giving them some duplication between the video and the text. So let's talk about just give you a brief outline of what should be on this page. First off, you identify the problem that they have. The transformation that needs to be made because it's based on a problem, they've got something that they're dealing with in their life that they've been not able to solve. You then talk about how that problem affects them, what it's holding them back from achieving in their life. Then you tell your hero's journey story. And if you go back to the modules where we talked about how to put together the webinar itself, you'll recognize many of the pieces here are mirroring what we talked about there. So we've identified the problem, we've shown how that affects them, we've told told your hero's journey story, and then we introduced the product. And then from there on out, we basically follow the same sales formula that we used in the webinar. So the second half of the webinar, where we're going through the offer process, we're doing basically everything from that. With the exception of we don't do a fast action bonus, we don't need to give them a URL because they're on the on the sales page right now, we don't do offer questions, we don't do other questions. Now we will probably want to put on an FAQ on that page. But we're not actually opening up to questions because this is a print version of a sales page. And I just want to bring something up because it's not absolutely clear from what you're seeing here is you definitely are going to want to include several testimony blocks in the sales letter. And then all of a sudden there's a block, probably a different color, a different background behind it, which has several testimonials in. So you break the pattern by giving testimonials, you go back into the offer process. ## [09:13 - 12:18]

others around it, a different background color, one of them might have an image that gets pushed back. So transparent or an overlay of another color on top of it, something so that you can still read text on top of that. But you use visual elements and graphic design elements to break up a sales letter so that people will actually not get bored and stop reading it. And then for each section of the sales letter, use a headline for each section to draw people through the sales letter and into that particular portion of the sales letter that immediately follows after that. Realize that people don't read sales letters initially, they skim. So here's what happens. When someone comes to a sales letter, they start at the top and usually they go all the way to the bottom to see how much costs see if they can afford it. Then they'll scroll back up and then they'll skim down the page. But also at that very first process, they're skimming as they go, they're seeing headlines past them. So if you don't have those headlines, they're not going to see them. And so they're going to skim the entire letter, they're going to see, oh, cool, and they'll stop and they'll pause and they'll read that little section, they'll skim down, do, do, do, do, do, do, oh, I'm going to read that little section. So they might read maybe two or three sections on your entire sales letter. And then if it's working for them, and they still haven't made the decision to buy, then they'll go up to the top and they'll actually read it. Now they'll probably skip some parts that feel boring to them. And that's just fine, because a different person is going to skip a different part. So we put them all in. But if we don't design a sales page to be skimmed, then we lose that key mid step, which is the step where they decide, oh, this is worth reading. That's why you have to put in these headlines, and this graphical breaking up to cause people to make the decision that it's worth reading the whole thing to learn what's in it. You'll also want to make sure you clearly provide buy buttons and the price so that people can see it. One of the things I see all the time when I look at people's data from their launch, I'll see a huge amount of people clicking to the buy page. So they click on the buy button to get to the buy page, and they immediately leave and they leave all together. Usually when that's happening is because the price was not clearly enough portrayed on the sales page. So you definitely want to have that price clearly there. Have buy buttons that are clearly there, easy to find, easy to click on different colors. So the buy button probably that's the only time you're going to use that color on the entire page. You do not want them to meld into the background. You do not want to have a classic graphics designer put those buttons in because they'll want it to look nice. And buttons are things you want to look not nice. You want them to jump out

## [12:18 - 12:48]

and to stand out because that's the thing you want them to do. So you've seen hundreds of sales letters. I don't need to tell you a lot about how to do this.
